    def set_exception(self, exception):
        """Mark the future done and set an exception.

        If the future is already done when this method is called, raises
        InvalidStateError.
        """
        if self._state != _PENDING:
            raise InvalidStateError('{}: {!r}'.format(self._state, self))
        if isinstance(exception, type):
            exception = exception() # <- all good, 
        self._exception = exception
        self._state = _FINISHED
        self._schedule_callbacks()
        if _PY34:

            # Arrange for the logger to be activated after all callbacks
            # have had a chance to call result() or exception().

Let me chime in on this one as I was not super familiar with cloudfront signing before I looked at the implementation. I also think we should just only allow for the custom/canned policy in the signature and then build abstractions like policy building on top of the signer. Here are the reasons why I think it is appropriate design choice:

1) Sure there are only 3 parameter combinations that cloudfront allows currently, but what stops them from adding more? Everytime they add a new parameter, we will have to go back and update the signer class because it involves the building of the custom policy, and we should not need to because that class's job is strictly to generate urls. This causes to be really a maintenance burden, and has been a pain for us in the past.

2) Botocore is the low-level library. The underlying classes and methods like the signers are not about being super user friendly. They are about being very robust, constant, and reliable. This allows us to build abstractions on top of these classes in other libraries like the CLI and boto3 that are much more user friendly (such as building the policy for the user).

I would recommend taking any of the policy building and move it to a CloudFrontPolicyBuilder class that exists in another module in botocore or lives in a library like the CLI, boto3, or a even new package and leave all of the signing logic in your signer class.

Relevant part from pep8 (emphasis mine):

>CapitalizedWords (or CapWords, or CamelCase -- so named because of the bumpy look of its letters [3] ). This is also sometimes known as StudlyCaps.

> Note: **When using abbreviations in CapWords, capitalize all the letters of the abbreviation**. Thus HTTPServerError is better than HttpServerError.

well, just returning the initialized reference will allow you to run the same function on any part of the code, something like:

```python
def add_items_to_references(reference=None, **kwargs):
    reference = {} if reference is None else reference
    for field, value in kwargs.iteritems():
        if value:
           reference[field] = value

        return reference
```

that way you can reuse it with an existing reference or just call it to create one (that's not so nice actually... as the name does not reflect that behavior very well though), like this:

```python
myref = {'something': 234}
myref = add_items_to_reference(myref, item1=123, item2='something else',...)

# or directly with
myref = add_items_to_reference(
    something=234,
    item1=123,
    item2='something else'
)
```

I suspect that most users who'd want to evaluate the futures returned (to act on errors) would want to bulk-process them (and with `concurrent.futures` some facilities for that [come provided][0]) in a pattern roughly like so:
```python
# Warning: potential pseudo-code ahead
pending = []
while True:
    for _ in xrange(big_number):
        msg = rawdata_consumer.consume()
        result = do_stuff_with_msg(msg)
        future = nextstage_producer.produce(result)
        pending.append(future)
    timeout = 0  # ie don't wait, just grab what's been delivered
    done, not_done = concurrent.futures.wait(pending, timeout)
    errors = [f.exception() for f in done if f.exception() is not None]
    pending = not_done
```

... and so, one might argue that if that's going to be the pattern, you ought to abstract the pattern into the producer interface, to save everyone the hassle of writing it all the time. On the other hand, it's not a terribly big or complicated pattern, and not baking it into the interface is more flexible, and we can always add convenience-wrappers for this later? So... I guess I'm starting to lean the way of just returning a future (but not a `handlers.ResponseFuture`, which would lack all the stuff around it).

[0]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html#concurrent.futures.wait

I'm quite happy with this, but when we discussed this "magic" in the context of my SLIC PR, the conclusion was that it's better to have a user flag, such as `rgb=True`, that determines whether an image is interpreted as 3D or 2D+c. The rationale was provided by @JDWarner: if someone has a little test 3D image with, say, `image.shape[-1] == 5`, on which they are developing their whole code, they will do the NLmeans denoising and be happy with their code. Then when they deploy it on many test images, they might come across a 3D image with `image.shape[-1] == 3`, and the code will do something completely different. In the case of a kwarg, users are protected from such a scenario.

The flag name is `multichannel` for SLIC and although it only makes much sense with RGB here, I would suggest we maintain the same flag name across all functions.

Also, right now, 3D images with exactly 4 columns in the last dimension just fail. =P

The lambda is just as a input argument filter to the DatagramSocket::ConnectAsync, StreamSocket::ConnectAsync, DatagramSocket::BindEndpointAsync, or StreamSocketListener::BindEndpointAsync APIs.

These APIs can validate all the input parameters themselves but they are supporting both Ipv6 and Ipv4. I don't want Ipv4 support in this case because it is Ipv6 only in Thread. That is why I want to filter something out.

I don't want it to be a public static method named like ContainOnlyValidIpv6Chars because the name is misleading. This method name is too generic. This function is intended to be just a filter function for input parameter(s) of those specific APIs only.

Another little benefit
A lambda could be optimized easily by the compiler. (Tested under VC++ 2015 / Release / x64)
